Why we moved our notes into plain Markdown files

For years our team kept its knowledge base inside a proprietary application that promised search, sync, and a tidy interface. It delivered on all three, right up until the day we wanted to leave. Exporting the data produced a tangle of HTML that no longer resembled the notes we had written, and the embedded attachments were gone entirely.

Plain Markdown files solved that problem in the most boring way possible. Each note is a file on disk that any editor can open, any backup tool can copy, and any program can read without a special library. When the storage format is just text, the data outlives the tool that produced it, which is exactly the property a long-lived archive needs.

The migration took an afternoon and the result has been quietly reliable ever since. We still use a server for search and a browser extension for clipping, but those are now conveniences layered on top of files we own, rather than a cage around data we merely rent.
